title: Fission
objectives: Describe nuclear fission and explain why heavy nuclei (A > ~90) can release energy by fission.
Explain the chain reaction in a fission reactor and the role of critical mass.
Describe the components of a nuclear fission reactor: fuel, moderator, control rods, coolant, and shielding.
Calculate the energy released in a fission reaction using E = mc² and binding energy per nucleon data.
Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of nuclear fission as an energy source compared to fossil fuels.
Explain the problem of radioactive waste and outline methods for its safe disposal.
summary:
Nuclear fission is the splitting of a heavy nucleus (typically uranium-235 or plutonium-239) into two smaller daughter nuclei (fission fragments) plus typically two or three fast neutrons and a large amount of energy, usually as kinetic energy of the fragments and gamma radiation. Fission is energetically favourable for heavy nuclei because those nuclei sit on the right-hand side of the binding energy per nucleon curve, where BE/A ≈ 7.6 MeV; the fission products sit near the peak (around iron, BE/A ≈ 8.8 MeV). Because the products are more tightly bound than the original nucleus, mass is converted to energy: ΔE = Δm × c². A typical fission of U-235 releases approximately 200 MeV per event, compared with a few eV per atom in a chemical reaction — roughly 50 million times more energy per kg of fuel.

A chain reaction occurs when each fission event releases neutrons that can trigger further fissions. For a self-sustaining (critical) reaction, the average number of neutrons that trigger a new fission per fission event (the multiplication factor k) must equal 1. If k < 1 (sub-critical), the reaction dies out; if k > 1 (super-critical), the reaction grows exponentially (as in a bomb). The minimum mass of fissile material required for a self-sustaining chain reaction is the critical mass, which depends on geometry, purity, and the presence of a reflector. In a reactor, k is kept at exactly 1 by controlling the neutron population.

A nuclear fission reactor contains several key components. Fuel rods contain enriched uranium (typically 3–5% U-235, compared with 0.7% in natural uranium) or plutonium. The moderator (often water, heavy water, or graphite) slows fast neutrons to thermal (slow) neutrons, which are far more likely to be captured by U-235 and cause fission; slowing is achieved through elastic collisions with nuclei of similar mass. Control rods (boron or cadmium) absorb neutrons; inserting them deeper into the core reduces k and lowers power output; withdrawing them raises power. In an emergency, control rods are fully inserted to shut down the reactor (SCRAM). The coolant (water, CO₂, or liquid sodium) carries heat away from the core to a heat exchanger and turbine. Thick concrete and steel shielding around the reactor absorbs gamma radiation and fast neutrons, protecting workers and the public.

The energy released in fission is calculated from the mass defect: Δm = m(reactants) − m(products), where masses are in atomic mass units (u; 1 u = 931.5 MeV/c²). Then ΔE = Δm × c² = Δm × 931.5 MeV. For example, for ²³⁵₉₂U + ¹₀n → ¹⁴¹₅₆Ba + ⁹²₃₆Kr + 3¹₀n, the mass defect is approximately 0.186 u, giving ΔE ≈ 0.186 × 931.5 ≈ 173 MeV. Additional energy comes from the kinetic energy of the neutrons and gamma emission, totalling ~200 MeV.

Advantages of nuclear fission: very high energy density (millions of times more than fossil fuels per kg), no direct CO₂ emissions during operation, reliable base-load power, and relatively small land area required. Disadvantages: radioactive waste remains hazardous for thousands of years; risk of catastrophic accidents (Chernobyl, Fukushima); high construction and decommissioning costs; uranium mining has environmental impacts; proliferation risk of fissile material for weapons. Radioactive waste is classified as low-level, intermediate-level, or high-level. High-level waste (spent fuel rods) must be stored in cooling ponds initially, then vitrified (encased in glass), and eventually placed in deep geological repositories (DGR) in stable rock formations.

quiz.question: Explain why uranium-235 can release energy when it undergoes fission, using the binding energy per nucleon curve.
quiz.markscheme: The binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) curve shows that heavy nuclei such as U-235 have BE/A ≈ 7.6 MeV, which is less than the BE/A of their fission products (typically in the range A ≈ 90–140, where BE/A ≈ 8.4–8.8 MeV, near the peak at iron). Because the products are more tightly bound (higher BE/A), the total nuclear binding energy of the system increases. This increase in binding energy is released as the mass defect: ΔE = Δm × c², where Δm = m(reactants) − m(products). A typical fission releases approximately 200 MeV per event.

quiz.question: Explain the role of the moderator and control rods in a nuclear fission reactor.
quiz.markscheme: Moderator: Fission of U-235 produces fast neutrons (kinetic energy ~1 MeV), which have a low probability of causing further fissions of U-235. The moderator (e.g. water, graphite) slows these neutrons to thermal energies (~0.025 eV) through repeated elastic collisions with nuclei of similar mass (most effective when the mass of moderator nuclei ≈ mass of neutron). Slow (thermal) neutrons have a much higher cross-section for absorption by U-235, sustaining the chain reaction efficiently. Control rods: Made of neutron-absorbing material (boron or cadmium), they are inserted into the reactor core to absorb excess neutrons. Inserting control rods reduces the neutron population and decreases the reaction rate (reduces k toward <1); withdrawing them allows k → 1 (criticality). Full insertion shuts down the reactor in an emergency.

quiz.question: A fission reaction produces a mass defect of 0.190 u. Calculate the energy released in MeV and in joules. (1 u = 931.5 MeV/c²; 1 MeV = 1.60 × 10⁻¹³ J)
quiz.markscheme: Energy in MeV: ΔE = Δm × 931.5 = 0.190 × 931.5 = 176.985 ≈ 177 MeV. Energy in joules: ΔE = 177 × 1.60 × 10⁻¹³ = 2.83 × 10⁻¹¹ J. This illustrates the enormous energy density of nuclear fuel; 1 kg of U-235 (~2.56 × 10²⁴ atoms) releases on the order of 8 × 10¹³ J, compared with ~3 × 10⁷ J per kg for coal.

quiz.question: Discuss two advantages and two disadvantages of using nuclear fission to generate electricity, compared with burning fossil fuels.
quiz.markscheme: Advantages: (1) Very high energy density — nuclear fuel releases millions of times more energy per kg than fossil fuels, so fuel requirements are tiny. (2) Zero CO₂ emissions during reactor operation — no greenhouse gases produced directly, unlike combustion of coal, oil, or gas. Disadvantages: (1) Radioactive waste — spent fuel rods contain highly radioactive isotopes with half-lives of thousands of years; storage in deep geological repositories is costly, and failure could contaminate groundwater. (2) Risk of serious accident — failure of cooling systems can cause core meltdown (as at Fukushima 2011), releasing radioactive material over a wide area, with long-term health and environmental consequences. Any two well-explained points from each side merit full marks.

quiz.question: Explain what is meant by critical mass and describe the condition for a self-sustaining chain reaction.
quiz.markscheme: The multiplication factor k is the average number of neutrons from each fission event that go on to produce a further fission. For a self-sustaining chain reaction, k = 1 (critical condition): on average exactly one neutron per fission causes a new fission, maintaining a constant reaction rate. If k < 1 (sub-critical), the chain reaction dies; if k > 1 (super-critical), the number of fissions grows exponentially. Critical mass is the minimum mass of fissile material needed to achieve k = 1 for a given geometry and purity. Below the critical mass, too many neutrons escape from the surface before causing fission; above it, enough are retained within the material to sustain the chain. Factors affecting critical mass: enrichment of fissile material, geometry (sphere has minimum surface-to-volume ratio), presence of a neutron reflector.

quiz.question: Outline the problem of high-level nuclear waste disposal and describe one long-term strategy for managing it.
quiz.markscheme: High-level nuclear waste (HLW) consists mainly of spent fuel rods and liquid waste from reprocessing. It contains fission products and actinides (e.g. plutonium, americium) with half-lives ranging from years to millions of years. HLW is intensely radioactive and thermally hot; it must be isolated from the biosphere until it decays to safe levels (potentially 10 000+ years). Strategy: Deep Geological Repository (DGR) — HLW is first stored in cooling ponds for several years, then vitrified (mixed with molten glass and solidified into glass blocks), which immobilizes the radioactive material. The glass blocks are then sealed in corrosion-resistant metal canisters and placed in tunnels or chambers drilled 300–1000 m deep into stable, impermeable rock (e.g. granite, clay). The tunnels are backfilled with bentonite clay (absorbs water, swells to seal gaps). The repository provides multiple barriers (engineered + geological) to prevent migration of radionuclides into groundwater.

title: Fusion and stars
objectives: Describe nuclear fusion and explain why light nuclei (A < ~56) can release energy by fusion.
Explain the conditions required for nuclear fusion and the challenges of achieving controlled fusion on Earth.
Describe the proton–proton chain as the main energy source in stars like the Sun.
Apply the Hertzsprung–Russell diagram to describe the life cycle of stars of different masses.
Calculate the energy released in a fusion reaction using E = mc² and binding energy per nucleon data.
Discuss nuclear fusion as a potential future energy source, including the role of tokamak reactors.
summary:
Nuclear fusion is the combining of two light nuclei to form a heavier nucleus. Fusion releases energy when the product nucleus has a higher binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) than the reactants — this is the case for nuclei with A less than about 56 (the iron peak). The most energetically favourable terrestrial fusion reaction is between deuterium (²H) and tritium (³H): ²₁H + ³₁H → ⁴₂He + ¹₀n + 17.6 MeV. The helium-4 nucleus carries ~3.5 MeV and the neutron ~14.1 MeV. This reaction has the highest cross-section at achievable temperatures.

Fusion requires extremely high temperatures (T ≥ 10⁸ K, i.e. ~10 keV) to give nuclei enough kinetic energy to overcome the Coulomb (electrostatic) repulsion barrier between their positive charges and approach within the range of the strong nuclear force (~10⁻¹⁵ m). In addition, the plasma must be confined at sufficient density for long enough (Lawson criterion: n τ_E ≥ ~10²⁰ m⁻³ s for D–T fusion). In stars, gravitational confinement at the core provides both the pressure and temperature needed. On Earth, magnetic confinement (tokamak reactors, using strong toroidal magnetic fields to contain the plasma) and inertial confinement (laser implosion of fuel pellets) are the main approaches. The ITER project in France is the world's largest experimental tokamak, aiming to demonstrate Q > 1 (more fusion energy out than heating energy in).

In stars with masses similar to the Sun, the dominant energy-producing process is the proton–proton (pp) chain. In three steps: (1) p + p → ²₁H + e⁺ + v_e (two protons fuse to form deuterium, emitting a positron and a neutrino); (2) ²₁H + p → ³₂He + γ; (3) ³₂He + ³₂He → ⁴₂He + 2p. The net result is 4¹₁H → ⁴₂He + 2e⁺ + 2v_e + 2γ, releasing approximately 26.7 MeV per helium-4 nucleus produced. The Sun converts approximately 4 × 10⁹ kg of mass into energy per second (E = mc²), producing luminosity L_⊙ ≈ 3.85 × 10²⁶ W. In more massive stars (main sequence: M > ~1.3 M_⊙), the CNO cycle dominates.

The Hertzsprung–Russell (HR) diagram plots stellar luminosity (L, in solar luminosities or absolute magnitude) against surface temperature (T_eff, in kelvin — note the x-axis runs right to left, from hot blue stars to cool red stars). Stars spend most of their lives on the main sequence, a diagonal band running from hot, luminous blue stars (upper left) to cool, dim red dwarf stars (lower right). Luminosity and temperature are related for main sequence stars by the mass–luminosity relation L ∝ M³·⁵ and the Stefan–Boltzmann law L = 4πR² σ T⁴. A star's position on the main sequence is determined by its mass.

When a star exhausts hydrogen in its core, it leaves the main sequence. For a Sun-like star: core contracts and heats → hydrogen shell burning begins → outer layers expand and cool → star becomes a red giant (upper right of HR diagram). Eventually the helium core undergoes helium flash (fusion of He → C, O via triple-alpha process: 3⁴₂He → ¹²₆C + γ). After helium exhaustion, the outer envelope is expelled as a planetary nebula, leaving a white dwarf (lower left, very hot but dim, cooling over billions of years — no fusion). For massive stars (M > ~8 M_⊙): after red supergiant phase, iron core forms (iron is the most stable nucleus — no energy can be released by fusion or fission). The core collapses in milliseconds, triggering a supernova explosion. The remnant is a neutron star (very dense, ~10 km radius) or, for the most massive cores (M_core > ~3 M_⊙), a black hole. Supernovae are the primary source of elements heavier than iron in the universe.

Fusion as an energy source offers compelling advantages: fuel (deuterium from seawater, tritium bred from lithium) is effectively limitless; no long-lived radioactive waste (the main product is helium-4; tritium has T½ = 12.3 years; neutron-activated reactor components are radioactive but for far shorter times than fission waste); no risk of runaway chain reaction (if confinement fails, the plasma simply cools and fusion stops); no CO₂ emissions. The key engineering challenges are achieving and sustaining plasma temperatures above 10⁸ K, meeting the Lawson criterion, managing neutron damage to reactor walls, and achieving a net energy gain (Q > 1, ideally Q >> 1 for commercial viability).

quiz.question: Explain why fusion of light nuclei releases energy, using the concept of binding energy per nucleon.
quiz.markscheme: The binding energy per nucleon (BE/A) curve has a maximum at iron (A ≈ 56, BE/A ≈ 8.8 MeV). For nuclei lighter than iron, BE/A increases as A increases. When two light nuclei fuse to form a heavier product closer to the peak, the product has a higher BE/A than the reactants. Because the product is more tightly bound, the total nuclear binding energy of the system is greater; this increase in binding energy equals the energy released, given by ΔE = Δm × c², where Δm is the mass defect (mass of reactants minus mass of products). For deuterium–tritium fusion, approximately 17.6 MeV is released per reaction.

quiz.question: State the conditions required for nuclear fusion to occur and explain why these conditions are difficult to achieve on Earth.
quiz.markscheme: Conditions: (1) Very high temperature — T ≥ ~10⁸ K (about 10 keV) so that nuclei have sufficient kinetic energy to overcome the Coulomb repulsion barrier and approach within ~10⁻¹⁵ m (range of strong force). (2) High plasma density and sufficient confinement time — described by the Lawson criterion: n τ_E ≥ ~10²⁰ m⁻³ s for D–T. Difficulties on Earth: no solid material can contain plasma at 10⁸ K (any contact would cool the plasma and melt the walls); magnetic confinement (tokamak) uses strong magnetic fields to keep plasma from touching walls, but sustaining stable confinement at the required density and temperature simultaneously is extremely challenging; energy breakeven (Q ≥ 1) has not yet been achieved in a sustained manner for practical power generation.

quiz.question: Describe the proton–proton chain in the Sun and calculate the energy released when four protons fuse to form one helium-4 nucleus. (m_p = 1.007276 u; m_He-4 = 4.002602 u; m_e = 0.000549 u; 1 u = 931.5 MeV/c²)
quiz.markscheme: The pp chain net reaction is: 4¹₁H → ⁴₂He + 2e⁺ + 2v_e + 2γ (plus the annihilation energy from the two positrons meeting two electrons: 2e⁺ + 2e⁻ → 4γ). Mass of 4 protons: 4 × 1.007276 = 4.029104 u. Mass of He-4: 4.002602 u. Mass of 2 positrons: 2 × 0.000549 = 0.001098 u. Total mass of products: 4.002602 + 0.001098 = 4.003700 u. Mass defect: Δm = 4.029104 − 4.003700 = 0.025404 u. Energy: ΔE = 0.025404 × 931.5 = 23.66 MeV. Including annihilation of 2 positrons with 2 electrons: 4 × 0.000549 × 931.5 = 2.04 MeV additional. Total ≈ 26.7 MeV. Steps of the chain: (1) p + p → ²H + e⁺ + v_e; (2) ²H + p → ³He + γ; (3) ³He + ³He → ⁴He + 2p (requiring steps 1 and 2 twice).

quiz.question: Describe the main sequence and explain what determines a star's position on the Hertzsprung–Russell diagram during the main sequence phase.
quiz.markscheme: The main sequence is a diagonal band on the HR diagram (luminosity vs. surface temperature) from upper-left (hot, luminous, blue-white O and B stars) to lower-right (cool, dim, red M dwarf stars). A star lies on the main sequence while it is fusing hydrogen to helium in its core — the longest phase of its life. Its position is determined primarily by its mass: more massive stars have higher core temperature and pressure, higher fusion rate, much higher luminosity (L ∝ M³·⁵), and higher surface temperature. The Stefan–Boltzmann law L = 4πR² σ T⁴ links luminosity, radius, and surface temperature. More massive main-sequence stars are larger, hotter, more luminous, and are situated higher-left on the HR diagram; less massive stars are smaller, cooler, less luminous, and situated lower-right.

quiz.question: Compare the end stages of a star similar in mass to the Sun with the end stages of a star of mass 15 M_⊙.
quiz.markscheme: Sun-like star (1 M_⊙): When hydrogen in the core is exhausted, the core contracts and heats while hydrogen shell burning expands the outer envelope → red giant. Helium core eventually reaches ~10⁸ K, triggering helium fusion (triple-alpha: 3⁴He → ¹²C + γ) — helium flash. After helium exhaustion, the star cannot fuse carbon (insufficient mass for higher temperatures). Outer layers are shed as a planetary nebula; the core remains as a white dwarf (electron-degenerate matter, ~Earth size, no fusion, slowly cooling). Massive star (15 M_⊙): Undergoes successive fusion stages (H → He → C → Ne → O → Si → Fe) in an onion-shell structure. Iron core cannot release energy by fusion or fission (maximum BE/A). When iron core exceeds the Chandrasekhar limit (~1.4 M_⊙), it collapses catastrophically in ~0.1 s → core bounce → supernova Type II explosion. The remnant is a neutron star (neutron-degenerate matter, ~10 km radius, ~10¹⁷ kg m⁻³) or, if core mass exceeds ~3 M_⊙, a black hole.

quiz.question: Discuss nuclear fusion as a potential future energy source, identifying two advantages over fission and two engineering challenges that remain to be solved.
quiz.markscheme: Advantages over fission: (1) Fuel supply is essentially unlimited — deuterium is extracted from seawater (1 in ~6400 hydrogen atoms is deuterium; oceans contain ~10⁴³ deuterium nuclei), and tritium can be bred from lithium in the reactor blanket. Fission requires mining limited uranium ore. (2) Radioactive waste is far less problematic — the main fusion product is helium-4 (inert, stable). Tritium has T½ = 12.3 years and decays to manageable levels within decades; neutron activation of reactor components produces isotopes with half-lives of years to decades, not thousands of years as with fission waste. Engineering challenges: (1) Achieving plasma temperatures above 10⁸ K and simultaneously satisfying the Lawson criterion (n τ_E ≥ 10²⁰ m⁻³ s) in a stable, sustained configuration — plasma instabilities (e.g. kink, sausage) disrupt confinement and quench the plasma. (2) Materials challenge — 14.1 MeV neutrons from D–T fusion bombard the reactor wall, causing radiation damage, embrittlement, and activation (making structural materials radioactive). No material has yet been demonstrated to withstand this neutron flux for the lifetime of a commercial reactor.
